topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper lay on the worn wooden table, the ink of the DNA results stark and undeniable. Elias stared at the lines, his breath catching in a shallow hitch. Each genetic marker felt like a verdict, a cold, undeniable truth that chipped away at the fragile scaffolding of his self-perception. He traced a finger over a section, the texture of the paper rough beneath his skin, and a wave of profound emptiness washed over him. He didn't move to tear it up; instead, he simply folded it once, slowly, deliberately, tucking it into the deepest pocket of his jeans as if sealing away a terrible secret. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming in his chest, a rhythm entirely out of sync with any sense of peace he’d managed to cultivate.

[story 2]

"So, this is it," Sarah murmured, her voice thin, barely audible over the hum of the refrigerator. She kept running her hand through her hair, the motion jerky and agitated. When the final report slid across the counter, she didn't look at it immediately. She just leaned against the marble, her shoulders slumping as if an invisible weight had settled upon them. Her gaze drifted to the window, where the city lights blurred into streaks of indifferent color. A low, ragged sound escaped her throat—not a sob, but something drier, more brittle. She picked up the document, her knuckles turning white against the glossy surface, and then dropped it onto the floor with a soft thud, the sound echoing the sudden, hollow drop in her stomach.

[story 3]

I sat on the edge of my bed, the blanket pooling around my legs like a defeated tide. The results were laid out, a litany of shared histories I never knew I lacked. My hands trembled slightly as I picked up the testing kit, the plastic cool against my palm. Every comparison, every percentage point, felt like a microscopic accusation. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to physically block out the overwhelming sensation of being fundamentally unanchored. When I finally opened them, the room seemed too bright, too loud. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space three times before collapsing back onto the mattress, the exhaustion of fighting something invisible settling deep into my bones.
